# Clean Architecture (Robert C. Martin)

Before writing, modifying, or planning any code, you MUST use your `read_file` tool to load the relevant guidelines from `.roo/vendor/`. You may read multiple files if the task crosses domains.

## Table of Contents:
    - [What Is Design and Architecture?](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/01-what-is-design-and-architecture.md): The generation, refactoring, evaluation, or planning of the architecture, design, or structural integrity of software systems, including the management of legacy code, technical debt, and the trade-off between code quality and development speed.
    - [A Tale of Two Values](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/02-a-tale-of-two-values.md): Generating new features, refactoring existing code, making architectural choices, and evaluating trade-offs between implementing quick functional fixes (behavior) and maintaining structural integrity (architecture).
    - [Paradigm Overview](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/03-paradigm-overview.md): These rules MUST activate whenever the AI is tasked with generating new code, refactoring existing software structures, establishing architectural boundaries, defining module algorithms, or managing data state and access.
    - [Structured Programming](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/04-structured-programming.md): This rule set is activated when the AI is tasked with system architecture design, algorithm implementation, code refactoring, module/component definition, or writing and structuring automated tests. It applies whenever the AI must translate requirements into structural code elements, organize logic, or verify software behavior.
    - [Object-Oriented Programming](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/05-object-oriented-programming.md): Designing system architecture, structuring object-oriented code, managing source code dependencies, creating or refactoring modules/components, defining class relationships, or isolating high-level business rules from low-level details (e.g., UI, Database, I/O devices).
    - [Functional Programming](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/06-functional-programming.md): Writing, refactoring, or reviewing code involving concurrency, multithreading, state management, algorithmic loops, functional programming paradigms (e.g., Lisp, Clojure, or functional features in Java/other languages), or data storage architectures, with a specific focus on event sourcing, race condition debugging, or the decoupling of stateful and stateless components.
    - [SRP: The Single Responsibility Principle](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/07-srp-the-single-responsibility-principle.md): The rule set activates whenever the AI is tasked with creating new modules, designing class architectures, refactoring existing source files, reviewing pull requests, or evaluating code cohesion, specifically when processing classes or modules that contain multiple methods, handle data structures, or serve requests originating from different types of users, departments, or stakeholders.
    - [OCP: The Open-Closed Principle](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/08-ocp-the-open-closed-principle.md): architecture design, system refactoring, component structuring, and dependency management tasks, specifically involving adding new features, extending system behavior, altering data presentation/storage, or drawing boundary lines between business rules and peripheral concerns.
    - [LSP: The Liskov Substitution Principle](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/09-lsp-the-liskov-substitution-principle.md): Designing class hierarchies, implementing interfaces, creating polymorphic methods, establishing REST API contracts across multiple services, or refactoring conditional logic that relies on type checking, service provider names, or hardcoded API route variations.
    - [ISP: The Interface Segregation Principle](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/10-isp-the-interface-segregation-principle.md): Designing class structures, defining interfaces, managing source code dependencies, refactoring coupled components, or integrating external architectural frameworks and libraries, applicable to both micro-level class design and macro-level architectural component selection.
    - [DIP: The Dependency Inversion Principle](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/11-dip-the-dependency-inversion-principle.md): Creating, refactoring, or modifying system architecture boundaries, module dependencies, object instantiation logic, or class inheritance structures, specifically involving cross-module function calls or object creations in both statically and dynamically typed languages.
    - [Components](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/12-components.md): Defining system architecture, organizing project directory structures, modularizing monolithic codebases, establishing build and deployment pipelines, configuring package managers (e.g., Maven, RubyGems, NuGet), or designing plugin systems and dynamic module loaders.
    - [Component Cohesion](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/13-component-cohesion.md): Software architecture design, specifically the grouping of classes, functions, or modules into larger components, packages, or deployable units during system refactoring, package creation, dependency management, and release cycle planning.
    - [Component Coupling](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/14-component-coupling.md): Architectural design, module organization, package dependency management, refactoring of existing component structures, resolving circular dependencies, or reviewing system-level build and deployment diagrams, specifically focusing on macro-level structural organization (components, packages, namespaces, shared libraries) rather than micro-level class implementation.
    - [What Is Architecture?](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/15-what-is-architecture.md): This rule file is activated whenever the AI is tasked with high-level system design, defining directory structures, separating modules, refactoring legacy code for better decoupling, choosing or implementing technology stacks (databases, web frameworks, delivery mechanisms), or designing deployment and team-collaboration topologies.
    - [Independence](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/16-independence.md): Architectural design, system restructuring, repository initialization, refactoring of dependencies, separating business logic from UI/Database details, dividing a monolithic codebase into modules or microservices, or resolving code duplication across different functional boundaries.
    - [Boundaries: Drawing Lines](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/17-boundaries-drawing-lines.md): Trigger these rules when defining system architecture, organizing project directories, establishing module boundaries, managing cross-component dependencies, designing interfaces between business logic and external systems (databases, UI, web servers), or setting up new frameworks within an application.
    - [Boundary Anatomy](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/18-boundary-anatomy.md): Architectural design, component modeling, defining module interfaces, structuring monoliths, extracting micro-services, configuring inter-process communications (IPC), or establishing any boundary interactions between separated software elements.
    - [Policy and Level](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/19-policy-and-level.md): Architectural design, component structuring, module organization, dependency management, refactoring of data flows, and defining the relationships between business logic and input/output (I/O) mechanisms, specifically governing source code dependencies and component boundaries.
    - [Business Rules](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/20-business-rules.md): The business logic, domain models, application core features, entities, and use cases, characterized by the separation of Critical Business Rules (which exist independently of a computer system) and application-specific business rules from external concerns such as databases, user interfaces, or third-party frameworks.
    - [Screaming Architecture](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/21-screaming-architecture.md): Triggers when the AI is tasked with generating project scaffolds, defining system directory structures, designing core business logic (Entities and Use Cases), integrating third-party frameworks, handling web/UI delivery mechanisms, or writing unit tests for business rules.
    - [The Clean Architecture](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/22-the-clean-architecture.md): Designing system architecture, restructuring or refactoring code bases, implementing separation of concerns, defining software layers, establishing module dependencies, or writing features that cross boundaries between business logic, user interfaces, and external systems (such as databases or frameworks).
    - [Presenters and Humble Objects](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/23-presenters-and-humble-objects.md): Generating, refactoring, or reviewing code that interacts with architectural boundaries, including User Interface (UI) development, presentation logic, database access layers, repository implementations, external service integrations, and unit testing for components interacting with I/O, UI, databases, or external frameworks.
    - [Partial Boundaries](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/24-partial-boundaries.md): Architectural design, component boundaries, dependency management, and the implementation of placeholder or lightweight boundaries between software components, particularly when balancing YAGNI (You Aren't Going to Need It) with anticipatory design for future scaling.
    - [Layers and Boundaries](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/25-layers-and-boundaries.md): Software architecture design, component modeling, boundary identification, API definition, refactoring existing architectures, evaluating system scaling strategies, or decoupling system modules.
    - [The Main Component](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/26-the-main-component.md): The entry point of a software system, including main functions, application bootstrappers, dependency injection composition roots, and environment-specific configuration loaders.
    - [Services: Great and Small](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/27-services-great-and-small.md): Evaluating, refactoring, designing, or implementing Service-Oriented Architectures (SOA), micro-services, distributed execution units, or when tasked with adding cross-cutting features to a system composed of multiple services.
    - [The Test Boundary](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/28-the-test-boundary.md): Creating, modifying, or analyzing test files, test suites, testing architecture, or test environments, including unit tests, integration tests, functional tests, BDD/TDD processes, and the relationship, boundaries, and dependencies between production code and test code.
    - [Clean Embedded Architecture](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/29-clean-embedded-architecture.md): Embedded systems, Internet of Things (IoT) devices, firmware, C/C++ hardware-interfacing applications, Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) integrations, or any software system that interacts directly with physical hardware or specialized microprocessors.
    - [The Database Is a Detail](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/30-the-database-is-a-detail.md): Designing, implementing, refactoring, or reviewing data access layers, database integrations, ORM usage, repository patterns, or any code that bridges business logic/use cases with data persistence technologies.
    - [The Web Is a Detail](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/31-the-web-is-a-detail.md): The architectural boundary and set of rules governing the separation between an application's core business logic (Use Cases) and its delivery mechanisms or peripheral details (such as the Web, GUI, or other IO devices), ensuring device independence and protecting the core logic from UI changes.
    - [Frameworks Are Details](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/32-frameworks-are-details.md): Framework integration and architectural boundary management, specifically focusing on the isolation of core business logic (Entities and Use Cases) from external frameworks, dependency injection configuration, and the scaffolding of application architecture.
    - [Case Study: Video Sales](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/33-case-study-video-sales.md): Architectural design, system partitioning, use case analysis, component architecture definition, deployment unit planning, or managing source code dependencies for a new or existing software product.
    - [The Missing Chapter](.roo/vendor/typescript/clean-architecture-robert-c-martin/34-the-missing-chapter.md): Designing system architecture, creating package or folder structures, defining module boundaries, setting access modifiers (e.g., public, private, internal, package-protected), refactoring monolithic codebases, or implementing separation of concerns in object-oriented programming languages (specifically Java, C#, or similar languages with package/assembly level visibility).
